examples.title: Basic Interrupt Handling Simulation
examples.description: Demonstrates fundamental interrupt mechanism - how CPU suspends normal execution to handle an interrupt request (IRQ), executes the interrupt service routine (ISR), and returns to normal execution.
examples.code:
# Basic Interrupt Handling Simulation
import time

# CPU State
class CPU:
    def __init__(self):
        self.pc = 0
        self.registers = [0] * 8
        self.running = True
        self.interrupt_enabled = True
        self.interrupt_flag = False
    
    def execute_instruction(self, instr):
        """Execute a single instruction"""
        print(f"[CPU] Executing: {instr}")
        time.sleep(0.5)
        self.pc += 1
    
    def handle_interrupt(self, irq_num):
        """Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)"""
        print(f"[INTERRUPT] IRQ {irq_num} received!")
        print(f"[CPU] Saving PC={self.pc} to stack")
        saved_pc = self.pc
        print(f"[CPU] Jumping to interrupt vector table")
        print(f"[CPU] Executing ISR for IRQ {irq_num}...")
        time.sleep(1)
        print(f"[CPU] Restoring PC={saved_pc}")
        self.pc = saved_pc
        self.interrupt_flag = False
        print("[INTERRUPT] Handler complete, resuming normal execution\n")

# Simulated program
cpu = CPU()
program = [
    "MOV R1, 10",
    "MOV R2, 20",
    "ADD R3, R1, R2",
    "SUB R4, R3, 5",
    "MUL R5, R4, 2"
]

# Main execution loop
print("=== Starting Program Execution ===\n")
cpu.execute_instruction(program[cpu.pc])
cpu.execute_instruction(program[cpu.pc])

# Simulate interrupt occurring
print("\n--- External Event: Timer Interrupt ---")
cpu.interrupt_flag = True
cpu.handle_interrupt(0)

# Continue execution
cpu.execute_instruction(program[cpu.pc])
cpu.execute_instruction(program[cpu.pc])
print("\n=== Program Complete ===")
examples.explanation: This example simulates basic interrupt handling: (1) CPU executes instructions sequentially, (2) An external interrupt (timer) sets the interrupt flag, (3) CPU checks flag after each instruction, (4) If interrupt detected, current PC is saved to stack, (5) Control transfers to ISR, (6) After ISR completes, PC is restored and normal execution resumes.

examples.title: 5-Stage Pipeline with Interrupt Handling
examples.description: Simulates a classic 5-stage RISC pipeline (IF-ID-EX-MEM-WB) and demonstrates what happens when an interrupt occurs mid-pipeline - showing instruction flush and PC recovery.
examples.code:
# 5-Stage Pipeline with Interrupt Handling
import time
from collections import deque

# Pipeline stages
STAGES = ["IF", "ID", "EX", "MEM", "WB"]

class PipelinedCPU:
    def __init__(self):
        self.pipeline = {stage: None for stage in STAGES}
        self.pc = 0
        self.stalled = False
        self.flush_requested = False
        self.instructions_executed = 0
    
    def fetch_instruction(self, program):
        """IF Stage: Fetch instruction"""
        if self.pc < len(program):
            instr = program[self.pc]
            self.pc += 1
            return instr
        return None
    
    def pipeline_clock(self, program, clock_num):
        """Simulate one clock cycle of pipeline"""
        print(f"\n--- Clock {clock_num} ---")
        
        # Shift pipeline (bubble up)
        for i in range(len(STAGES)-1, 0, -1):
            self.pipeline[STAGES[i]] = self.pipeline[STAGES[i-1]]
        
        # Fetch new instruction
        self.pipeline["IF"] = self.fetch_instruction(program)
        
        # Display pipeline state
        for stage in STAGES:
            instr = self.pipeline[stage]
            if instr:
                print(f"  {stage}: {instr}")
        
        # Track completion
        if self.pipeline["WB"]:
            self.instructions_completed += 1
            print(f"  >> Instruction completed: {self.pipeline['WB']}")

    def handle_interrupt(self, clock):
        """Handle interrupt in pipeline - requires flush"""
        print(f"\n*** INTERRUPT at Clock {clock}! ***")
        print("[PIPELINE] Flushing all stages...")
        
        # Clear pipeline (simulate pipeline flush)
        for stage in STAGES:
            self.pipeline[stage] = None
        
        # Save PC (point to instruction that was in IF)
        saved_pc = self.pc
        print(f"[CPU] Interrupt: saved return PC = {saved_pc}")
        
        # Simulate ISR execution
        print("[CPU] Executing Interrupt Service Routine...")
        time.sleep(0.5)
        
        # After ISR, refetch the interrupted instruction
        self.pc = saved_pc
        print(f"[CPU] Returning, refetching from PC = {self.pc}")
        print("[PIPELINE] Pipeline refilling...\n")

# Sample program
program = [
    "ADD R1, R2, R3",
    "SUB R4, R5, R6",
    "MUL R7, R8, R9",
    "DIV R10, R11, R12",
    "LOAD R13, [R14]",
    "STORE R15, [R16]",
    "JUMP label",
    "AND R17, R18, R19"
]

# Run pipeline simulation
cpu = PipelinedCPU()
cpu.instructions_completed = 0

print("=== 5-Stage Pipeline Execution ===")
for clock in range(1, 13):
    # Simulate interrupt at clock 6
    if clock == 6:
        cpu.handle_interrupt(clock)
    
    cpu.pipeline_clock(program, clock)

print(f"\n=== Total Instructions Completed: {cpu.instructions_completed} ===")
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates pipeline interrupt handling: (1) Instructions flow through 5 stages each clock cycle, (2) At clock 6, an interrupt occurs, (3) Pipeline must be flushed - all in-progress instructions cleared, (4) ISR executes, (5) PC saved points to instruction that was being fetched, (6) After ISR, pipeline refills starting from the interrupted instruction. This shows the complexity of handling interrupts in pipelined processors.

examples.title: Multi-Stage Pipeline with Interrupt Prioritization and Stalls
examples.description: Advanced simulation showing priority-based interrupt handling, pipeline stalls (bubbles), and the concept of interrupt latency in a deep pipeline architecture.
examples.code:
# Advanced: Multi-Stage Pipeline with Priority Interrupts and Stalls
import time
from enum import IntEnum
from queue import PriorityQueue

class InterruptPriority(IntEnum):
    RESET = 0      # Highest priority
    NMI = 1        # Non-maskable interrupt
    HARDWARE = 2   # External hardware interrupt
    SOFTWARE = 3   # Software trap/interrupt
    TIMER = 4      # Lowest priority

class InterruptController:
    def __init__(self):
        self.pending_queue = PriorityQueue()
        self.in_service = None
        self.masked = False
    
    def raise_interrupt(self, priority, vector):
        """Queue an interrupt request"""
        self.pending_queue.put((priority, vector))
        print(f"[PIC] Interrupt raised: {InterruptPriority(priority).name} (vector={vector})")
    
    def get_highest_priority(self):
        """Get highest priority pending interrupt"""
        if self.masked or self.pending_queue.empty():
            return None
        if self.in_service:
            return None  # Interrupt nesting disabled
        priority, vector = self.pending_queue.get()
        self.in_service = (priority, vector)
        return (priority, vector)
    
    def complete_interrupt(self):
        """Mark interrupt as complete"""
        self.in_service = None
        print("[PIC] Interrupt service completed")

class PipelineWithStalls:
    def __init__(self):
        self.stages = ["IF", "ID", "EX", "MEM", "WB"]
        self.pipeline = {s: None for s in self.stages}
        self.stall_cycles = 0
        self.pc = 0
        self.cycles = 0
    
    def insert_bubble(self):
        """Insert a bubble (stall) in pipeline"""
        # Shift and insert NOP
        for i in range(len(self.stages)-1, 0, -1):
            self.pipeline[self.stages[i]] = self.pipeline[self.stages[i-1]]
        self.pipeline["IF"] = "NOP (stall)"
        self.stall_cycles += 1
    
    def execute_cycle(self, program, interrupt_ctrl):
        """Execute one pipeline cycle"""
        self.cycles += 1
        
        # Check for pending interrupts
        irq = interrupt_ctrl.get_highest_priority()
        if irq:
            priority, vector = irq
            print(f"\n>>> INTERRUPT ACCEPTED: {InterruptPriority(priority).name} <<<")
            
            # Insert 2 bubbles (simulate latency)
            print("[Pipeline] Inserting bubbles for interrupt latency...")
            self.insert_bubble()
            self.insert_bubble()
            
            # Handle ISR (simulated)
            print(f"[CPU] Handling ISR vector {vector}")
            
            # Clear pipeline after ISR
            self.pipeline = {s: None for s in self.stages}
            print("[Pipeline] Pipeline flushed and restarted\n")
            interrupt_ctrl.complete_interrupt()
            return
        
        # Normal execution: shift pipeline
        for i in range(len(self.stages)-1, 0, -1):
            self.pipeline[self.stages[i]] = self.pipeline[self.stages[i-1]]
        
        # Fetch next instruction
        if self.pc < len(program):
            self.pipeline["IF"] = program[self.pc]
            self.pc += 1
        else:
            self.pipeline["IF"] = None
        
        # Show pipeline
        print(f"Cycle {self.cycles}: ", end="")
        for stage in self.stages:
            instr = self.pipeline[stage] if self.pipeline[stage] else "-"
            print(f"{stage}:{instr[:8]:<8} ", end="")
        print()

# Program with potential hazards
program = [
    "LOAD R1, [0x100]",      # Memory hazard ahead
    "ADD R2, R1, R3",        # Depends on R1 (data hazard)
    "SUB R4, R5, R6",
    "MUL R7, R8, R9",
    "AND R10, R11, R12",
    "OR R13, R14, R15",
    "SHIFT R16, R17, 1",
    "JUMP loop"
]

# Initialize
cpu = PipelineWithStalls()
pic = InterruptController()

print("=== Pipeline with Interrupt Prioritization ===\n")

# Execute with interrupts at different cycles
for cycle in range(1, 20):
    # Simulate timer interrupt at cycle 8
    if cycle == 8:
        pic.raise_interrupt(InterruptPriority.TIMER, 0x20)
    
    # Simulate hardware interrupt at cycle 12
    if cycle == 12:
        pic.raise_interrupt(InterruptPriority.HARDWARE, 0x30)
    
    cpu.execute_cycle(program, pic)
    
    if cpu.pc >= len(program) and all(v is None for v in cpu.pipeline.values()):
        break

print(f"\n=== Statistics ===")
print(f"Total Cycles: {cpu.cycles}")
print(f"Stall Cycles: {cpu.stall_cycles}")
print(f"Pipeline Efficiency: {((cpu.cycles - cpu.stall_cycles) / cpu.cycles * 100):.1f}%")
examples.explanation: This advanced example demonstrates: (1) Priority-based interrupt controller using priority queue, (2) Higher priority interrupts can preempt lower ones, (3) Pipeline stalls/bubbles inserted during interrupt latency (simulating time to save state and transfer to ISR), (4) Pipeline flush after interrupt completion, (5) Multiple interrupts queued - timer at cycle 8 (lower priority) and hardware at cycle 12 (higher priority). Shows how interrupts introduce pipeline bubbles and reduce efficiency.
